content.text: CSS preprocessors extend CSS with variables, nesting, functions, and mixins that compile to plain CSS at build time. Sass (Syntactically Awesome Style Sheets) — specifically the
syntax — is the industry standard and Angular's built-in choice. This document covers Sass/SCSS features as they apply to Angular development, using the modern
module system that replaced the deprecated

attrs.alt: SCSS in Angular: compilation pipeline, variables, nesting, mixins, @use/@forward, built-in functions, and @extend

content.text: Plain CSS has improved significantly since preprocessors were introduced — CSS custom properties, nesting, and
now cover many original use cases. However, SCSS still provides value for:
— breakpoints, design scale ratios, and loop inputs that don't need to change at runtime
— reusable blocks of CSS with parameters (media query helpers, flex/grid patterns, BEM utilities)
— custom computation returning CSS values (e.g. fluid spacing scale)
— proper module system with namespacing, preventing global pollution
and import them only where needed
When generating a new project, specify SCSS as the stylesheet format:

# Install Dart Sass (already included in Angular CLI 14+)
npm install sass --save-dev
and are resolved at compile time. They are the right tool for values that parametrize your build output — breakpoints, scale multipliers, mixin arguments:
for runtime values. A breakpoint can be a SCSS variable (it never changes at runtime); a theme color should be a CSS custom property (it changes in dark mode).
SCSS nesting mirrors HTML structure and eliminates repetition. The
Deep nesting produces highly specific selectors that are hard to override and slow to match. If you need 4+ levels, restructure the component.
Mixins are reusable blocks of styles that can accept arguments. They are the SCSS equivalent of a function that outputs CSS:
is deprecated in Dart Sass 1.80+ and will be removed. Use
to import a module (with a namespace) and
to re-export from an index file:
SCSS functions return a single value and are called inline:
SCSS control directives generate CSS programmatically:
shares a set of rules between selectors by grouping them in the compiled output.
placeholders are never compiled unless extended:
Prefer mixins over `@extend` in Angular projects.
groups selectors in unexpected ways when combined with Angular's ViewEncapsulation scoping attributes. It can also produce very long selector lists. Mixins duplicate output but are predictable.
Dart Sass ships built-in modules that must be imported with

src/
  styles/
    _tokens.scss        // CSS custom property declarations + SCSS variables
    _typography.scss    // Font stacks, base type styles
    _breakpoints.scss   // Mixin: respond-to()
    _mixins.scss        // All reusable mixins
    _reset.scss         // Box-sizing + base reset
    _index.scss         // @forward all of the above
  styles.scss           // Global entry: @use 'styles'; sets body/html
  app/
    components/
      button/
        button.component.ts
        button.component.scss  // @use '../../styles' as s; uses tokens/mixins
Modern browsers support CSS nesting natively. The behaviour differs slightly from SCSS:
For new Angular projects with modern browser targets, CSS nesting reduces the build step for simple nesting cases. SCSS is still valuable for mixins,
utility generation, and the module system.
is deprecated in Dart Sass. It pollutes the global namespace and re-executes every file it's imported in. Migrate to
— generates specificity-heavy selectors that are hard to override. Prefer flat BEM naming.
— they can't change at runtime for dark mode. Use CSS custom properties for anything that varies by theme.
— can produce unexpected selector groupings with Angular's ViewEncapsulation attribute selectors. Prefer mixins.
legacy API and produce sRGB-gamut results. Use
— generates CSS that isn't used in every component. Prefer CSS custom properties + calc() for spacing utilities; use dedicated utility libraries if needed.
